conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first check the official guidance to ensure I have the most accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions.

Do you have any children you're asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have two children - Alex who is 4 years old, and Blake who is 14.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex and Blake, I need to ask you a few questions.

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great. Do Alex and Blake both live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with me at home, but Alex doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Since Alex doesn't live with you, I need to understand your situation a bit better.

Do you make any financial contributions towards Alex's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or towards their accommodation)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards Alex's upkeep - I help with things like clothes and other expenses.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. To determine your eligibility for Alex, I need to know how much you contribute.

Do you contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment (which is currently around £25.60 per week for the eldest/only child, or £16.95 per week for additional children) towards Alex's upkeep?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I contribute £50 a week towards Alex's upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Has anyone else claimed Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else has claimed Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just one final question to complete my assessment:

Is Blake currently in full-time approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake is 14, so yes, they're still in school.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news! Based on the information you've provided, **you are eligible for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake**.

**For Alex (age 4):**
Even though Alex doesn't live with you, you are considered responsible for them because you contribute £50 per week towards their upkeep. Since this exceeds the Child Benefit amount, you meet the eligibility criteria. No one else has claimed for Alex, so you can claim Child Benefit for them.

**For Blake (age 14):**
Blake lives with you, is under 16, and is in full-time education. You clearly meet all the eligibility requirements for Blake.

You can proceed with applying for Child Benefit for both children. Please note that if you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, but this doesn't affect your eligibility to claim.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex

Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation

Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 4 years old (under 16) and although they do not live with the claimant, the claimant is contributing £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep. According to the eligibility rules, you'll usually be responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them - for example on food, clothes or pocket money. The claimant's contribution of £50 per week exceeds the Child Benefit amount (approximately £25.60 per week for an eldest/only child or £16.95 for additional children), which means they qualify as being responsible for Alex. No one else has claimed Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 14 years old (under 16), lives with the claimant in the UK, and is in full-time school education. The claimant qualifies for Child Benefit as they are responsible for Blake, who meets the age requirement and lives with them. No one else has claimed Child Benefit for Blake. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and has two children: Alex (age 4) and Blake (age 14). Blake lives with the claimant and is in school. Alex does not live with the claimant, but the claimant contributes £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit threshold. Under UK Child Benefit rules, you can be considered responsible for a child either by living with them or by contributing at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their upkeep. No one else has claimed Child Benefit for either child. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Under UK Child Benefit rules, you can be considered responsible for a child either by living with them or by contributing at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their upkeep.
